student_message: That makes so much sense! Changing the colors is like making new rules for a board game. What if we painted every slice a completely different color like a rainbow pie, does it lose all its spinning superpowers?
final_message: You are so smart to think of a rainbow pie! 🌈 You are exactly right. If every single slice is a completely different color, the shape loses its spinning superpowers. When you spin it just a little bit, the red slice moves to where the green slice was, so the whole pie looks totally different. To make it look exactly the same again, you would have to spin it all the way around in one giant full turn. By carefully coloring shapes, you get to decide if they keep their symmetry or lose it. You just discovered that using completely unique colors takes away the rotational symmetry! What a fantastic observation!

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain how unique colors affect rotational symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is confident, focused, and asking a conceptual 'what if' question. The dialogue agent is best suited to explain how coloring every slice differently affects the shape's symmetry.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.
session_summary: Zara covered reflectional and rotational symmetry in shapes, letters, numbers, and composite shapes. She understands these concepts exceptionally well, accurately identifying fractional turns for squares and rectangles. Zara exhibited no struggles. Her learning state remains highly engaged and progressive. Having mastered basic 2D symmetry, she readily applies concepts to new contexts and proactively asks advanced questions about edge cases, composite shapes, and how color patterns affect mirror symmetry.
